July 21, 1982


Gordon C. Phillips
City Attorney
415 Diamond Street
Redondo Beach, CA  90277

Attn: Stuart B. Scudder
      Deputy City Attorney

Re: Your Request for Written Advice,
    Our No. A-82-111

Dear Mr. Phillips:

     Thank you for your letter requesting our advice
regarding certain conflicts of interest disqualification
issues involving the City of Redondo Beach Harbor Review
Board. The facts as you state them are as follows:

                         THE FACTS

     The City of Redondo Beach has established a board by
ordinance to perform certain governmental functions in the
beach and harbor areas of the city only. Some of the
functions are advisory with the City Council making the
final decision. Others are final subject to an appeal to
the City Council.

     An example of one of its advisory functions is
reviewing and recommending a leasing arrangement between
the city and a private developer. In the case of any lease
the Council must authorize its execution. However, since
the board is newly created, you cannot say whether the
board's recommendation will be routinely accepted.

     An example of the board's discretionary function is
granting conditional use permits in the harbor area.
Essentially, the board functions as the "planning
commission" for the city beach and harbor areas. Although
the board is not involved in zoning or general planning in
the harbor area, it does make important decisions affecting

Gordon C. Phillips
July 21, 1982
Page 2


individual developmental rights within its area of
jurisdiction. Such decisions are final subject to appeal
to the City Council.

The problem, which prompts this request, is that there
are three vacancies on this seven person board due to the
lengthy screening process for new members. Financial
conflict of interests have occurred involving the four
members who have been named to date. There is no provision
in the code for what happens if the board cannot obtain a
quorum to enable it to take action.

The questions, as you state them, are as follows:

THE QUESTIONS

1. Does the "rule of necessity" apply, when vacancies
could be filled by appointment by the City Council? Is
there any mandate on the Council to fill such vacancies to
avoid application of the "rule of necessity?"

2. Four members of the board is a quorum. Can a board
member with a conflict of interest participate under the
"rule of necessity" to form a quorum of the board, but
abstain from the vote without affecting the quorum?

3. Does the "rule of necessity" apply when the board
is exercising its advisory functions?

ANALYSIS

Government Code Section 871001/ prohibits a public
official from making or participating in making a
governmental decision in which he or she has a financial
interest. Section 87101 carves a very narrow exception
from that prohibition. It permits participation by an
official with a conflict of interest where that
participation is "legally required." Section 87101
specifically states that "the fact that an official's vote
is needed to break a tie does not make his participation
legally required."

_______________

1/ All statutory references made are to the Government
Code unless otherwise specified.

Gordon C. Phillips
July 21, 1982
Page 3


By regulation (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18701) (copy
enclosed) the Commission has further refined the concept of
"legally required participation." That regulation states
inter alia that participation is not legally required
"unless there exists no alternative source of decision
consistent with the purposes and terms of the statute
authorizing the decision." By its own terms, the
regulation is to be "construed narrowly."

We turn now to the analysis of the questions which you
have posed in your letter.

No. 1 Vacancies

Initially, it should be pointed out that "legally
required participation" and the "rule of necessity" are not
synonymous. This was discussed at length in the
Commission's Hudson Opinion.2/

It is our opinion that, under the specific facts
presented here, where the ordinance provides for seven
members, appointed by the mayor and confirmed by the City
Council, and where the appointing authority has simply
failed to fill the Board membership, the rule of legally
required participation does not apply.3/ The City Council
has recently enacted an ordinance establishing a Harbor
Review Board and now seeks to have that Board operate
before the City officials have completed its appointment.
This is insufficient reason for permitting a disqualified
member to participate where otherwise he or she could not
do so.

No. 2 Quorum

When a member of a board or commission is disqualified
from participation in a decision by the provisions of
Section 87100, he or she may not be counted toward

_______________________

2/ 4 FPPC Opinions 13, No. 77-007, Feb. 7, 1978, copy
enclosed.

3/ This is not necessarily true of all situations in
which vacancies may occur on appointive or elective bodies
and we confine our advice to the specific facts at hand.

Gordon C. Phillips
July 21, 1982
Page 4


achieving a quorum.4/ This is implicit in the Commission's
Hudson Opinion, supra, and in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18701(c)(2) (copy enclosed).

Therefore, the answer to your question is "no," a
disqualified member may not count toward a quorum and then
simply abstain from voting.

No. 3 Advisory Functions

The underlying question which you pose is: Do the
provisions of Section 87100 apply when the Board is
exercising its advisory functions? The exception of
"legally required participation" will apply only if
disqualification is first required by Section 87100. The
meaning of that section is explicated by the Commission's
regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700 (copy
enclosed). In subdivision (c), we have defined the term
"participates in the making of a governmental decision" to

4/ Black's Law Dictionary, Revised Fourth Ed., cites
under its definition of the term "quorum," the following
pronouncement:

to Such a number of the members of a body as is competent
transact business in the absence of the other members,
Morton v. Talmadge, 166 Ga. 620, 144 S.E. ill.

Corpus Juris Secundum, in discussing "quorum" states,
in pertinent part, as follows: "Members disqualified
because of interest cannot be counted for the purpose of
making a quorum or a majority of the quorum." 67A CJS 619
(Parliamentary Law Section 6), citing Enright v. Hecksher,
N.Y., 240 F. 863 (2d Cir. 1917). In Enright, the court
stated:

A director, whose interest in a matter dis-
qualifies him from voting upon a resolution concerning
it, cannot, according to the better opinion, be counted
for the purpose of ascertaining whether a quorum is
present when the vote is taken. A director so dis-
qualified by personal interest loses, pro hac vice, his
character as a director, and so cannot be counted.
That is the law of New Jersey. [Cite.] And it is
supported by the weight of authority in other
jurisdictions as well. [Cites.] Id., at 872-873.

Gordon C. Phillips
July 21, 1982
Page 5


include advising or making recommendations to the decision-
maker directly by preparing any report, analysis or opinion
whch requires the exercise of judgment and the purpose of
which is to influence the decision. Based upon the facts
here, we conclude that the Harbor Review Board meets this
test when engaging in its advisory functions as spelled out
in Sec. 12-1.908 of Ordinance No. 2327c.a. (copy
enclosed). Consequently, a member is "participating"
within the meaning of Section 87100.

We trust that this advise has been responsive to your
questions. Should you have any questions regarding this
letter, please call me at 916/322-5901.

Sincerely,

Robert E. Leidigh
Counsel
Legal Division

DB 3-281
ORDINANCE NO. 2327 c.s.
AN ORDINANCE OF THE CITY COUNCIL
OF THE CITY OF REDONDO BEACH
ADDING ARTICLE 9 TO CHAPTER 1,
TITLE 12, OF THE REDONDO BEACH
MUNICIPAL CODE, RELATING TO THE
HARBOR REVIEW BOARD

The City Council of the City of Redondo Beach has determined
that it is necessary for the Municipal Code to provide for the
establishment of a Harbor Review Board and therefore finds
as follows:

1. That the orderly development of King Harbor requires
that the review of developmental proposals be reviewed by a decision-
making body cognizant of the needs of commercial/recreational
harbors.

2. That the review of developmental proposals must be accom-
plished in a manner consistent with the requirements of Section
66000 et seq. of the California Government Code.

3. That the review of developmental proposals within King
Harbor must be accomplished in a manner consistent with
the Coastal Element of the General Plan and the California
Coastal Act of 1976; and

4. That the establishment of a Harbor Review Board will
insure that developmental proposals within King Harbor are
consistent in manner specifics herein above.

NOW, THEREFORE, the City Council of the City of Redondo
Beach does ordain as follows:

CHAPTER 1, ARTICLE 9 OF THE CODE. Article 9 is hereby
added to Chapter 1, Title 12, of the Redondo Beach Municipal
Code as follows:

ARTICLE 9. HARBOR REVIEW BOARD

Sec. 12-1901. Created.

There is hereby created a Harbor Review Board (Board)
for the City of Redondo Beach.

Sec. 12-1902. Jurisdiction.

The jurisdiction of the Board shall be limited to the Harbor
area as delineated on a map at Sec. 12-1912 of this article.

Sec. 12-1903. Appointment and terms of members.

Members shall serve four (4) years commencing on
July 1 and ending on June 30 four years hence, except that
three of the individuals initially appointed to the Board shall
be appointed for shorter terms expiring on June 30, 1983, while
the other four members shall be appointed for terms expiring
on June 30, 1984. All members shall be appointed by
the Mayor subject to the confirmation of the City Council, as
set forth in Sec. 15-2 of the Charter of the City of Redondo
Beach, from among the following:

a. Two members selected at large
   or more members having knowledge or expertise in one
   or more of the following areas:
   i. Commercial real estate
   ii. Architecture and/or planning
   iii. Business and/or finance
   
c. One member shall be chosen from a list of nominees
presented to the City by a majority of those firms which have
a lease or sublease with the City of property within the jurisdiction
of this Board; provided, however, that no lessee, leaseholder or
employee of said firms shall be appointed to the Board
per Sec. 12-1904 failing vacancies.

If any vacancy shall occur in the membership of the Board,
other than by expiration of the term of office, it shall be filled
by the Mayor with the approval of the City Council for the
unexpired term of the vacated office.

Sec. 12-1905. Removal of members.

Any members of the Board may be removed from office
by the Mayor for cause with the approval of the City Council.

Sec. 12-1906. Appointment of a chairperson.

At the first meeting of the Board in every fiscal year, the
members of the Board shall appoint one of their number as
a chairperson and one of their number as a vice-chairperson,
both of whom shall hold office for one year and until his/her
successor is appointed.

Sec. 12-1907. Meetings and failure to attend.

a. The Board shall hold regular meetings at least once
every month.

b. In the event that any member of the Board shall absent
himself from the regular meetings of the Board for a period
of 50 days consecutively following the last regular meeting
attended by such member, unless by permission of the City
Council expressed in its official minutes, or in the event such
member shall be convicted of a crime involving moral turpitude,
or shall cease to be a resident of the City of Redondo
Beach, his office shall become vacant and shall be acted on
by the City Council.

Sec. 12-1908. Powers and duties.

a. The Board shall hold hearings on all applications for
Conditional Use Permits and Variances for the use and development of lands within the jurisdiction of the Board as provided
for in Sections 10-2.810 through 10-2.813, Article 2, Chapter 2,
Title 10, of this code.

b. The Board shall hold hearings on any matters concerning
the establishment of Conditional developments of the Harbor.

c. The Board shall further the commercial and recreational
development of the Harbor.

d. The Board shall review and recommend to the City
Council on all new or lease and sublease agreements with the
City relating to Harbor lands, facilities or improvements.

e. The Board shall be advised and shall report to the City
Council on all persons or firms doing business within the
jurisdiction of the Board.

f. The Board shall be advised and may report to the City
Council on the assignment of all leases and subleases with the
City relating to Harbor lands, facilities or improvements.

g. The Board shall advise the City Council on any matters
concerning the Harbor when so requested by the Council.

h. The Board shall make recommendations on proposed
Applications for a Conditional Use Permit and Variance
shall be filed with the Harbor Department and shall be processed
in the manner set forth in Section 10-2.1701, Article 6, Chapter
2, Title 10 of this code.

Sec. 12-1910. Finality of decision.

The decision of the Board shall be final, unless within 20
days after the Board's decision is approved, any aggrieved party
shall appeal the action taken by the Board to the City Council.
The City Council shall, after public hearing, sustain, modify,
reject, or overrule any action taken by the Board. The action
of the Council shall be final and conclusive.

Sec. 12-1911. Rules and regulations.

The Board shall establish and publish such rules and regulations as it deems necessary for its government and for the
conduct of its business.

SECTION 2. Any provision of the Redondo Beach Municipal
Code, or appendices thereto, or other ordinances of the
city inconsistent herewith, to the extent of such inconsistencies
and no further, are hereby repealed.

SECTION 3. If a section, subsection, sentence, clause, or
phrase of this ordinance or any other ordinance of the city
is for any reason held to be invalid
or unconstitutional by the decision of any court of competent
jurisdiction, such decision shall not affect the validity of the
remaining portions of this ordinance. The City Council hereby
declares that it would have passed this ordinance and each
section, subsection, sentence, clause and phrase thereof irrespective of the fact that any one or more sections, subsections,
sentences, clauses or phrases be declared invalid or unconstitutional.

SECTION 4. PUBLICATION AND EFFECTIVE DATE. This
ordinance shall be published in the Breeze, the official newspaper of said city, same in full once and operation thereafter
thirty (30) days after its final passage and adoption, except
that Section 12-1909 shall not be operative until ten (10) days
after official certification of this ordinance by the California
Coastal Commission as required by Local Coastal Program
Regulations, Section 00070.

Passed, approved, and adopted this 15th day of March, 1982.

BARBARA J. DOERR
Mayor

And in the same case the Court of Errors and Appeals, referring
to the New Jersey Corporation Act, declares that:

"The whole spirit and policy of the act are so clearly opposed to any arrangement by which corporate stock shall be issued without receiving by the
company of an equivalent in value to its par that any agreement to this effect
must be deemed void as contrary to the policy of the law. If any doubt has
existed upon this question it must be taken as settled by the decision of this
court in Valncy v. Nixon, 48 N. J. Eq. 609, 20 At. 1290, 70 N. J. Eq. 749, 64
Atl. 220, 8 L. R. A. (N. S.) 271, 10 Ann. Cas. 84."

[5] Moreover, under the New Jersey decisions, if the contract contravenes the Corporation Act, it is not voidable, but void, and no proceedings are necessary to procure an adjudication of its nullity. Feston National Bank v. American Brick & Tile Co., supra; Holcombe
v Trenton White City Co., supra. It therefore follows that the agreement that the stock purchased by Mumford should be regarded as fullpaid was void, and affords no obstacle to the relief asked in this suit.

The theory upon which the case was submitted was that, in case the
jury found that the value of the licenses was less than $1,000,000, they
should place a value upon them as of the time when the Syndicate took
them over, and that the difference between such value and $1,000,000
would represent the extent to which the $1,000,000 stock issue was
unpaid when it was turned back into the treasury; that one-half of
that difference would be the amount Heckscher would have to pay for
the one-half of the 10,000 shares he took, and that on that amount
he should be credited with $250,000 in cash, which it was conceded he
had paid, and that on the balance he should pay interest from November 8, 1905, that being the date when he acquired his stock.

His charge to the jury the learned District Judge, in referring
to the action of the directors in taking over the licenses for $1,000,000
of stock, informed them that:

"It seems from the minutes that there were five directors present, but that
Mr. Mumford was interested in this transaction, and left the room of retired
while the directors were considering this proposition, and took action upon
this proposition, and was not present when it was voted upon. Of course, it
would constitute a quorum if he who there even though he had not voted,
but it to left the room, so as to leave only 4 directors present, and if were
stated it seems similarly clear that there would have been no directors acting at the time this action was taken, and therefore there would have been
an inquiry by the directors, as required by section 49 of the Corporation Law,
that the value of the property transferred was equal to the value of the stock
issued."

[6] A director, whose interest in a matter disqualifies him from
voting upon a resolution concerning it, cannot, according to the better
opinion, be counted for the purpose of ascertaining whether a quorum
is present when the vote is taken. A director so disqualified by personal interest loses, pro hac vice, his character as a director, and so
cannot be counted. That is the law of New Jersey. Metropolitan
Telephone & Telegraph Co. v. Domestic Telegraph & Telephone Co.,
43 N. J. Eq. 626, 14 Atl. 908; Id., 44 N. J. Eq. 508, 14 Atl. 907. And
it is supported by the weight of authority in other jurisdictions as well.
Bassett v. Fairchild, 132 Cal. 637, 64 Pac. 1082, 52 L. R. A. 611;
Curtin v. Salmon River Hydraulic Gold Mining & Ditch Co., 130 Cal.

answered specifically by saying that section 20560 does not permit an agency to
eliminate a contractual provision and that section 20461.5 would prevent the city
from adopting the one fiftieth at fifty-five formula for new employees.

5. Nothing in the retirement law prevents an agency from amending its
contract to provide a greater retirement benefit for its employees. While section
20461.5 speaks of an election to provide an optional benefit as being irrevocable
the section has never been interpreted as barring an agency which adopted a
particular optional feature from later adopting another optional provision which
gives greater benefit. Thus, an agency could move its fire personnel from the
miscellaneous category which has a two percent at age sixty formula (§ 21251.15)
to the one fiftieth at age fifty-five formula (§ 21252.6) and then to a two percent
at age fifty benefit (§ 21252.01). It would be a most unreasonable construction
that would allow a city to move its fire personnel from the miscellaneous two
percent at age sixty formula to the one fiftieth at age fifty-five formula but would
preclude an agency already having the optional one fiftieth at age fifty-five formula
from doing so. The obvious purpose of the irrevocable provisions in section
20461.5 is to prevent an agency from substituting a lesser benefit in place of a
greater.

In answering question 5 it is assumed that the same benefit will be provided
for both present and future employees. It would not be possible under existing law
to distinguish between employees in the same group, for instance, employees
hired prior to January 1, 1975 as opposed to those hired after that date, even
though both classes would enjoy greater benefits.


Opinion No. CV 77-227—May 23, 1978

SUBJECT: CONFLICT OF INTEREST IN CITY REDEVELOPMENT—Both
the Health and Safety Code and the Government Code would be violated
if a bank were to purchase or lease property within a redevelopment project,
assuming any city councilman is a shareholder, officer, or director of the bank.

Requested by: SENATOR, 21ST DISTRICT

Opinion by: EVELLE J. YOUNGER, Attorney General
Geoffrey L. Graybill, Deputy

The Honorable Newton R. Russell, Senator for the 21st District, has
requested an opinion on the following questions:

1. Would section 33130 of the Community Redevelopment Law be violated
if a bank were to purchase or lease property within a redevelopment project area,
assuming the city councilmen or any of them are (a) shareholders, (b) officers,
or (c) directors of the bank?

2. Would section 1090 et seq. of the Government Code be violated should

MAY 1978:

ATTORNEY GENERAL'S OPINIONS

243

an existing bank which offers to purchase or lease property of a redevelopment
agency.

Applicability of Health and Safety Code Section 33130

Health and Safety Code section 33130 provides:

"No agency or community officer or employee who in the course of
his duties is required to participate in the formulation of or to approve
plans or policies for the redevelopment of a project area shall acquire
any interest in any property included within a project area within the
community. If any such officer or employee owns or has any direct or
indirect financial interest in such property, he shall immediately make
a written disclosure of it to the agency and the legislative body which
shall be entered on their minutes. Failure to so disclose constitutes mis-
conduct in office.

"This section shall not prohibit any such officer or employee from
acquiring an interest in property within the project area for the purpose
of participating as an owner or reentering into business pursuant to this
part provided that such officer or employee has owned a substantially equal
interest as that being acquired for the three years immediately preceding
the selection of the project area." (Emphasis added.)

The city council of a city which includes a redevelopment project area is
required to review and pass on a redevelopment plan for the project area. (Health
& Saf. Code § 33360 et seq.) In addition the city council in question has opted
pursuant to Health and Safety Code section 33309 to vest itself with the powers,
duties, privileges and immunities of the redevelopment agency. Therefore, as
provided by Health and Safety Code section 33130 a member of the city council
may not "acquire any interest in any property included within a project area within
the [city]. . . ." The question presented is whether a bank in which a member
of the council is a shareholder, director, or an officer may lease or purchase
property in the project area. An obvious answer to this question is that it is the
bank, assuming that it is a properly qualified, bona fide corporate entity, not a
member of the city council which acquires the interest in property within the
project area; therefore, Health and Safety Code section 33130 taken literally and
in isolation would not appear to prohibit these transactions.

However, section 33150 permits members of the council to retain "any direct
or indirect financial interest in such property" which was possessed before gaining
membership on the council. It may be concluded that "any interest" in property
which may not be acquired after becoming a member of the council includes the
concept "indirect financial interest" used in connection with property acquired
before becoming a member of the council. Section 33130 distinguishes between
what is permitted a member of the council and what is not on the basis of when an
interest is acquired, not on the basis of the nature of the interest in property. There
is no reason to assume that "any interest" in property in the acquisition section

MAY 1978]          ATTORNEY GENERAL'S OPINIONS          247

to prevent conflicts of interest in a member of a redevelopment agency with respect to property within the redevelopment area under the jurisdiction of that agency, a bank, a director or officer of which is a member of governing body of the redevelopment agency, may not acquire such property, or a lease thereof. This conclusion carries through on the mandate of judicial authority to interpret conflict of interest statutes broadly to avoid the possibility of divided loyalty or bias on the part of public officials.

B. Shareholders

However, both the people, in enacting the Political Reform Act of 1974, and the Legislature, in enacting Government Code section 1090 et seq., have recognized that not all financial interests are of sufficient magnitude to create the serious possibility of divided loyalty or bias. As noted supra at page 9, Government Code section 1091.5, subdivision (1)(a), establishes that a public official is not financially interested in a contract with a corporation if the official owns less than three percent of the shares of the corporation, provided that dividends from the corporation do not exceed five percent of the official's total annual income and that other payments from the corporation do not exceed five percent of the official's total annual income. The Political Reform Act of 1974 recognizes through Government Code section 87103, subdivision (a), that an investment in a business entity, which investment is worth $1,000 or less, does not create a conflict of interest in a public official.

An investor exceeding the possibly lower threshold established by the Political Reform Act of 1974 might result in the disqualification of an official from participation in making a decision; however, the existence of the investment alone would not invalidate governmental transactions with the business entity. (See Gov. Code §§ 87102, 91003.) In contrast, ownership by a government official of shares of a corporation in excess of three percent of the total will, pursuant to Government Code section 1092, invalidate any contracts with the corporation made by the official or a governmental body of which the official is a member. (See Sigall v. City of Taft, supra, 58 Cal. 2d at pp. 568-569; Terry v. Bender, supra, 143 Cal. App. 2d at p. 206.)

The effect of exceeding the threshold of Government Code section 1091.5, subdivision (1)(a), is similar to what results if Health and Safety Code section 33130 is applicable in the same circumstances. The effect is that a corporate entity is barred from a business opportunity which it may have had but for the

MAY 1978                ATTORNEY GENERAL'S OPINIONS                249

Government Code section 1090 provides that:

"Members of the Legislature, state, county, district, judicial district,
and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of
which they are members. Nor shall state, county, district, judicial district,
and city officers or employees be purchasers at any sale or vendors at any
purchase made by them in their official capacity.

"As used in this article, 'district' means any agency of the state
formed pursuant to general law or special act, for the local performance of
governmental or proprietary functions within limited boundaries."

This office has concluded that deposits of funds of a district agricultural
association in a bank violated section 1090 because a director of the association was
an officer and member of the board of directors of the bank. (24 Ops. Cal. Atty.
Gen. 200 (1954).) Since a member of a city council or of a redevelopment agency
would have the same status in the context of section 1090 as a director
of a district agricultural association, the member would have a financial interest
in a contract between the bank and the council for the sale or lease of real property
in a redevelopment project area. There is no exemption in Government Code section
1090 through 1091.5 from the prohibition of section 1090 for an officer or director
of a bank who is also a member of the city council which would be contracting with
the bank; therefore, such a transaction would be a violation of section 1090.

However, section 1091.5 specifically exempts a public official's status as a
shareholder of a contracting party from the ambit of section 1090 if the official owns
less than three percent of the shares and if the official's dividend income from those
shares is five percent or less of the official's total annual income and if all other
payments from the corporation to the official do not exceed five percent of the
official's total annual income.¹ It is clear in accordance with the doctrine of expressio
unius est exclusio alterius.

¹ Subdivision (b)(2) of section 1091 classifies as a "remote interest" the status of an
agent or employee of a contracting party with ten or more employees if the agent or employee
has not contracting for the government agency has been employed by the contracting party for
less years prior to taking the government position. Since the members of the council are
all in the process of forming the bank, this provision is clearly inapplicable. Subdivision
(b)(9) of section 1091 recognizes that an official of a government agency which contracts
with a "remote interest" in the transaction if the party with whom the government agency is
contracting is a creditor, debtor, depositor or borrower with a bank of which the government
official is a director or officer. Since the bank itself would be a party to the transaction, the
bank could not be considered "remote" pursuant to subdivision (b)(9). Subdivision (2) of
section 1091.5 is inapplicable to this transaction for the same reason.

Subdivision (2) of section 1091.5 provides that:

"An officer or employee shall not be deemed to be interested in a contract
pursuant to competitive bidding under a procedure established by law if his
sole interest is that of an officer, director, or employee of a bank or savings and loan
association with which a party to the contract has the relationship of borrower or
depositor, debtor or creditor."

if his interest is:

"(a) The ownership of less than 3 percent of the shares of a corporation for
profit, provided the total annual income to him from dividends, including the value

section 87100 et seq. (Gov. Code § 83111), has adopted a regulation which defines
what constitutes "the extent his participation is legally required for the action or
decision to be made" as stated in Government Code section 87101. The regulation
(2 Cal. Admin. Code § 18701, subd. (a)) provides that:

"(a) A public official is not legally required to make or to participate in the making of a governmental decision within the meaning of
Government Code Section 87101 unless there exists no alternative source
of decision consistent with the purposes and terms of the statute authorizing the decision."

Thus, no participation is legally required of an official unless there is no alternative
source of decision. In the context of a decision maker which is a collective body the
determination of whether participation in a decision by a member of the body is
legally required involves two phases of analysis. The first phase is a determination
of whether there is any alternative to the collective body as a source of decision
which does not present a conflict of interest situation.

The decision which may be made in this instance is whether or not certain
property controlled by the redevelopment agency shall be sold or leased to a bank.
Health and Safety Code section 33432² requires that a redevelopment agency lease
or sell most of the property which it has acquired in a project area. Since the
powers of a redevelopment agency "are vested in the members [of the agency] in
office" (Health & Saf. Code § 33121), the city council, which has acted pursuant
to Health and Safety Code section 33200 to make itself the redevelopment agency,
has the duty to lease or sell property which it has acquired in a project area.
Although a redevelopment agency is authorized pursuant to Health and Safety Code
section 33202 to establish a community redevelopment commission to which it
could delegate agency functions, we are informed by the city attorney for the city
in question that the city council has created no such commission. Therefore, the
city council, acting as the redevelopment agency, is the only authority capable of
selling or leasing agency property³ which must be disposed of pursuant to Health
and Safety Code section 33432.

Having determined that there is no alternative to the city council as a source
of decision on entering into contracts of sale or lease of property in a redevelopment
project area, the second phase of analysis focuses upon the members of the council
who are financially interested in a particular sale or lease. The issue is whether the
participation of a member or members of the city council in a decision, which must
be made and in which such member or members are financially interested, is legally

MAY 1, 1789]       ATTORNEY GENERAL'S OPINIONS       283

when a member or members of a body, who are present and who are necessary to
constitute a quorum, fail to vote.

The most important consideration in determining what participation is
legally required of a member of the city council sitting as the redevelopment agency
is to give effect to the policy manifested in Government Code section 87101. (See
Select Base Materials v. Board of Equal. (1959) 51 Cal. 2d 640, 645.) Government
Code section 87101 appears to reflect a compromise between two conflicting policies. One such policy which is recognized in the law, is that members of public
bodies have a duty to vote on issues before them so that the public is represented
and receives the services that the public bodies were created to provide. As observed
by the court in Dry Creek Valley Assn., Inc. v. Board of Supervisors, supra, at
page 844:

"... [there is] the public policy that members of public legislative
bodies take a position, and vote, on issues brought before them. This
policy has been expressed as 'the duty of members of a city council to vote
and that they ought not "by inaction prevent action by the board.'
(Citation omitted.) Or as elsewhere stated: 'The duty to vote is present
if the member is present' (Citation omitted.)"

This public policy is so strong that it has prevailed to permit a body, which was
the only entity capable of acting, to act where all of its members have a financial
interest in the decision. See Gonsalev v. City of Davis Valley (1968) 265 Cal.
App 2d 400, 404, wherein the court stated:

"The rule is well settled that where an administrative body has a duty
to act upon a matter which is before it and is the only entity capable to act
in the matter, the fact that the members may have a personal interest in
the result of the action taken does not disqualify them to perform their
duty. It is a rule of necessity which has been followed consistently.
(Thompson v. City of Long Beach, 41 Cal. 2d 235, 243-244 [259 P.2d
649]; Caminetti v. Pacific Mut. L. Ins. Co., 22 Cal 2d 314, 366 [139 P.2d
908]; Jeffry v. City of Salinas, 232 Cal. App. 2d 29, 40 [42 Cal. Rptr.
486]; Alvis v. County of Fresno, 178 Cal. App. 2d 443, 452 [2 Cal. Rptr.
779].)"

The rule of necessity is inconsistent with the policy manifested in Government
Code section 87100 which requires that a public official with a financial interest
in a decision be precluded from participation in making it. Section 87100 is
designed to protect the public interest in unbiased decision making. (See generally,
Terry v. Bender, supra, 143 Cal. App. 2d at pp. 206-208; People v. Eliot, supra,
115 Cal. App. 2d at pp. 418-419.) If the policy of avoiding conflicts of interest
which is reflected in Government Code section 87100 were given full rein, some
decisions which public bodies were created to make would not be made. If the rule
of necessity were given full rein the policy of avoiding conflicts of interest would
be completely nullified. Government Code section 87101 compromises the two

flict of interest statutes broadly to avoid the possibility of divided loyalty or bias on 
the part of public officials. 
B. Shareholders 
However, beth Ehe people, in enacting the Political Reform Act of 1974, and 
the Legislature, in enilcting Government Code section 1090 et seq., have recognized 
that not aU financial interests are of sufficient magnitude to create the serious 
possibility of divided loyalry or bias. As noted jlflfra at page 9, Government Code 
section 1091.5, subdivision (1)( a), establishes that a public official is not finan-
cially interested in a contract with a corporation if the official owns less than three 
percent of the shares of the corporation, provided that dividends from the corpora-
tion do not exceed five percent of the official's total annual income and that other 
payments from the corporation do not exceed five percent of the official's tOtal 
annual income. The Political Reform Act of 19-4 recognizes through Government 
Code section 87103, subdivision (a), that an investment in a business entity, 
which investment is worth $1,000 or less, does not create a conflict of interest in a 
public official. 
An investment exceeding the possibly lower threshold established by the 
Political Reform Act of 1974 might result in the disqualification of an official from 
participation in making a decision; however, the existence of the investment alone 
would not invalidate governmental transactions with the business entity. (See 
Gov. Code §§ 87102,91003.) In contrast, ownership by a government official of 
shares of a corporation in excess of three percent of the total will, pursuant to 
Government Code section 1092, invalidate any contracts with the corporation made 
by the official or a governmental body of which the official is a member. (See 
Stigall v. Cily of Tafl, supra, 58 Cal. 2d at pp. 568-569; Terry v. Bendel-, supra, 
143 Cal. App. 2d at p. 206.) 
The effect of exceeding the threshold of Government Code section 1091.5, 
subdivision (1) (a), is similar to what results if Health and Safety Code section 
33130 is applicable in the same circumstances. The effect is that a corporate 
entity is barred from a business opportunity which it may have had but for the

The decision which may be made in this instance is whether or not certain 
property controlled by the redevelopment agency shaH be sold or leased to a bank. 
Health and Safety Code section 33432: requires that a redevelopment agency lease 
or sell most of the property which it has acquired in a project area. Since the 
powers of a redevelopment agency "are vested in the members [of the agency] in 
office" (Health & Saf. Code § 33121), the city council, which has acted pursuant

has the duty to lease or sell property which it has acquired in a project area. 
A.lthough a redevelopment agency is authorized pursuant to Health and Safety Code

ATTORNEY GENERAL'S OPINIONS 
The FPPC has decided that the preferred means of selecting an interested 
member to participate would be by lot or some other means of random selection 
but other impartial and equitable means of selection are not precluded. (4 FPPC 
Opinions 13,17-18 (No. 77-007, February 8,1978).) We concur with the views 
of the FPPC. 
It should be noted that the foregoing discussion regarding the applicability 
of Government Code section 87101 is relevant to a decision on a contractual matter 
such as the lease or sale of redevelopment agency property only where no conBict 
of interest exists pursuant to Government Code section 1090 el seq. but does 
exist pursuant to-povernment Code sections 87100 and 87103. Such a circumstance 
may occur in the context of facts considered herein if the only conflict of interest 
situation arises where a council member owns shares of a corporation to an extent 
which is permitted by Government Code section 1091.5, subdivision (1) (a) <' ~ 
(sNpra, at p. 9) but which creates a conflict of interest pursuant to Government 
Code section 87103, subdivision (a) (supra, at pp. 5-6). As noted in footnote 5, 
supra, at page 9, while the provisions of Government Code section 87100 el seq. 
might operate to permit a governmental entity to enter into a contract in which one 
of its members is financially interested, the contract would be prohibited if • 
conflict of interest exists pursuant to Government Code section 1090 el seq.lZ (See 
59 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 604,617-619.) It has been and is the opinion of this office 
that pursuant to Government Code section 81013 the provisions of Government 
Code section 87100 el seq. did not supersede Government Code section 1090 el seq. 
(See 59 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 604, 617.) Therefore, if a conBict of interest regarding 
a contract of sale or lease of redevelopment agency property exists in a member of 
the city council pursuant to Government Code section 1090 el seq., the contract is 
prohibited regardless of Government Code section 87100 el seq. A willful Violation 
of the provisions of section 1090 el seq. is punishable pursuant to Government Code 
section 1097, which provides that: 
"Every officer or person prohibited by the laws of this state from J 
making or being interested in contracts, or from becoming a vendor or 
purchaser at sales, or from purchasing scrip, or other evidences of in-
debtedness, including any member of the governing board of a school 
district, who willfully violates any of the provisions of such laws, is pun-
ishable by. fine of not more than one thousand doUars ($1,000), or by 
imprisonment in the state prison, and is forever disqualified from holding 
any office in this state."

sections.analysis:
Government Code Section 871001/ prohibits a public 
official from making or participating in making a 
governmental decision in which he or she has a financial 
interest. 
Section 87101 carves a very narrow exception 
from that prohibition. 
It permits participation by an 
otficial with a conflict of interest where that 
participation is "legally required." 
Section 87101 
specifically states that "the fact that an official's vote 
is needed to break a tie does not make his participation 
legally required." 
1/ All statutory references made are to the Government 
Code-unless otherwise specified. 


Gordon C. Phillips 
July 21, 1982  
By regu14tion (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18701) (copy 
enclosed) the Commission has further refined the concept of 
"legally required participation." 
That regulation states 
inter alia that participation is not legally required 
"unless there exists no alternative source of decision 
consistent with the purposes and terms of the statute 
authorizing the decision." 
By its own terms, the 
regulation is to be "construed narrowly." 
We turn now to the analysis of the questions which you 
have posed in your letter. 
No. 1 Vacancies 
Initially, it should be pointed out that "legally 
required participation" and the "rule of necessity" are not 
synonymous. 
This was discussed at length in the 
Commission's Hudson Opinion.l/ 
It is our opinion that, under the specific facts 
presented here, where the ordinance provides for seven 
members, appointed by the mayor and confirmed by the City 
Council, and where the appointing authority has simply 
failed to fill the Board membershi~, the rule of legally 
required participation does not apply.3/ 
The City Council 
has recently enacted an ordinance establishing a Harbor 
Review Board and now seeks to have that Board operate 
before the City officials have completed its appointment. 
This is insufficient reason for permitting a disqualified 
member to participate where otherwise he or she could not 
do so. 
No. 2 Quorum 
When a member of a board or commission is disqualified 
from participation in a decision by the provisions of 
Section 87100, he or she may not be counted toward 
2/ 
4 FPPC Opinions 13, NO. 77-007, Feb. 7, 1978, copy 
enclosed. 
3/ 
This is not necessarily true of all situations in 
which vacancies may occur on appointive or elective bodies 
and we confine our advice to the specific facts at hand. 


Gordon C. Phillips 
July 21, 1982  
achieving a quorum.4/ 
This is implicit in the Commission's 
Hudson Opinion, supra, and in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18701 (c)' (2) 
(copy enclosed) . 
Therefore, the answer to your question is "no," a 
disqualified member may not count toward a quorum and then 
simply abstain from voting. 
No.3 Advisory Functions 
The underlying question which you pose is: 
Do the 
provisions of Section 87100 apply when the Board is 
exercising its advisory functions? 
The exception of 
"legally required participation" will apply only if 
disqualification is first required by Section 87100. 
The 
meaning of that section is explicated by the Commission's 
regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700 (copy 
enclosed). 
In subdivision (c), we have defined the term 
"participates in the making of a governmental decision" to 
4/ Black's Law Dictionary, Revised Fourth Ed., cites 
under its definition of the term ",quorum," the following 
pronouncement: 
Such a number of the members of a body as is competent 
to transact business in the absence of the other members; 
Morton v. Talmadge, 166 Ga. 620, 144 S.E. 111. 
Corpus Juris Secundum, in discussing "quorum" states, 
in pertinent part, as follows: 
"Members disqualified 
because of interest cannot be counted for the purpose of 
making a quorum or a majority of the quorum." 
67A CJS 619 
(Parliamentary Law Section 6), citing Enright v. Hecksher, 
N.Y., 240 F. 863 (2d Cir. 1917). 
In Enright, the court 
stated: 
A director, whose interest in a matter dis-
qualifies him from voting upon a resolution concerning 
it, cannot, according to the better opinion, be counted 
for the purpose of ascertaining whether a quorum is 
present when the vote is taken. 
A director so dis-
qualified by personal interest loses, pro hac vice, his 
character as a director, and so cannot be counted. 
That is the law of New Jersey. 
[Cite.] 
And it is 
supported by the weight of authority in other 
jurisdictions as well. 
[Cites.] 
Id., at 872-873. 


Gordon C. Phillips 
July 21, 1982  
include advising or making recommendations to the decision-
maker directly by preparing any report, analysis or opinion 
whch requires the exercise of judgment and the purpose of 
which is to influence the decision. 
Based upon the facts 
here, we conclude that the Harbor Review Board meets this 
test when engaging in its advisory functions as spelled out 
in Sec. 12-1.908 of Ordinance No. 2327c.a. (copy 
enclosed). 
Consequently, a member is "participating" 
within the meaning of Section 87100. 
We trust that this advise has been responsive to your 
questions.

B. Shareholders 
However, beth Ehe people, in enacting the Political Reform Act of 1974, and 
the Legislature, in enilcting Government Code section 1090 et seq., have recognized 
that not aU financial interests are of sufficient magnitude to create the serious 
possibility of divided loyalry or bias. As noted jlflfra at page 9, Government Code 
section 1091.5, subdivision (1)( a), establishes that a public official is not finan-
cially interested in a contract with a corporation if the official owns less than three 
percent of the shares of the corporation, provided that dividends from the corpora-
tion do not exceed five percent of the official's total annual income and that other 
payments from the corporation do not exceed five percent of the official's tOtal 
annual income. The Political Reform Act of 19-4 recognizes through Government 
Code section 87103, subdivision (a), that an investment in a business entity, 
which investment is worth $1,000 or less, does not create a conflict of interest in a 
public official. 
An investment exceeding the possibly lower threshold established by the 
Political Reform Act of 1974 might result in the disqualification of an official from 
participation in making a decision; however, the existence of the investment alone 
would not invalidate governmental transactions with the business entity. (See 
Gov. Code §§ 87102,91003.) In contrast, ownership by a government official of 
shares of a corporation in excess of three percent of the total will, pursuant to 
Government Code section 1092, invalidate any contracts with the corporation made 
by the official or a governmental body of which the official is a member. (See 
Stigall v. Cily of Tafl, supra, 58 Cal. 2d at pp. 568-569; Terry v. Bendel-, supra, 
143 Cal. App. 2d at p. 206.) 
The effect of exceeding the threshold of Government Code section 1091.5, 
subdivision (1) (a), is similar to what results if Health and Safety Code section 
33130 is applicable in the same circumstances. The effect is that a corporate 
entity is barred from a business opportunity which it may have had but for the

Thus, no participath>n is legally required of an official unless there is no alternative

legally required involves twO phases of analysis. The first phase is a determination 
of whether there is any alternative to the collective body as a source of decision 
which does not present a conflict of interest situation. 
The decision which may be made in this instance is whether or not certain 
property controlled by the redevelopment agency shaH be sold or leased to a bank. 
Health and Safety Code section 33432: requires that a redevelopment agency lease 
or sell most of the property which it has acquired in a project area. Since the 
powers of a redevelopment agency "are vested in the members [of the agency] in 
office" (Health & Saf. Code § 33121), the city council, which has acted pursuant 
to Health and Safety Code section 33200 to make itself the redevelopment agency, 
has the duty to lease or sell property which it has acquired in a project area. 
A.lthough a redevelopment agency is authorized pursuant to Health and Safety Code 
section 33202 to establish a community redevelopment commission to which it 
could delegate agency functions, we are informed by the city attorney for the city 
in question that the city council has created no such commission. Therefore, the 
city council, acting as the redevelopment agency, is the only authority capable of

and Safety Code section 33432.

The FPPC has decided that the preferred means of selecting an interested 
member to participate would be by lot or some other means of random selection 
but other impartial and equitable means of selection are not precluded. (4 FPPC 
Opinions 13,17-18 (No. 77-007, February 8,1978).) We concur with the views 
of the FPPC. 
It should be noted that the foregoing discussion regarding the applicability 
of Government Code section 87101 is relevant to a decision on a contractual matter 
such as the lease or sale of redevelopment agency property only where no conBict 
of interest exists pursuant to Government Code section 1090 el seq. but does 
exist pursuant to-povernment Code sections 87100 and 87103. Such a circumstance 
may occur in the context of facts considered herein if the only conflict of interest 
situation arises where a council member owns shares of a corporation to an extent 
which is permitted by Government Code section 1091.5, subdivision (1) (a) <' ~ 
(sNpra, at p. 9) but which creates a conflict of interest pursuant to Government 
Code section 87103, subdivision (a) (supra, at pp. 5-6). As noted in footnote 5, 
supra, at page 9, while the provisions of Government Code section 87100 el seq. 
might operate to permit a governmental entity to enter into a contract in which one 
of its members is financially interested, the contract would be prohibited if • 
conflict of interest exists pursuant to Government Code section 1090 el seq.lZ (See 
59 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 604,617-619.) It has been and is the opinion of this office 
that pursuant to Government Code section 81013 the provisions of Government 
Code section 87100 el seq. did not supersede Government Code section 1090 el seq. 
(See 59 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 604, 617.) Therefore, if a conBict of interest regarding 
a contract of sale or lease of redevelopment agency property exists in a member of 
the city council pursuant to Government Code section 1090 el seq., the contract is 
prohibited regardless of Government Code section 87100 el seq. A willful Violation 
of the provisions of section 1090 el seq. is punishable pursuant to Government Code 
section 1097, which provides that: 
"Every officer or person prohibited by the laws of this state from J 
making or being interested in contracts, or from becoming a vendor or 
purchaser at sales, or from purchasing scrip, or other evidences of in-
debtedness, including any member of the governing board of a school 
district, who willfully violates any of the provisions of such laws, is pun-
ishable by. fine of not more than one thousand doUars ($1,000), or by 
imprisonment in the state prison, and is forever disqualified from holding 
any office in this state."

Scudder Deputy City Attorney Re: Your Request for Written Advice, Our No. A-82-111 Dear Mr. Phillips: Thank you for your letter requesting our advice regarding certain conflicts of interest disqualification issues involving the City of Redondo Beach Harbor Review Board. The facts as you state them are as follows: THE FACTS The City of Redondo Beach has established a board by ordinance to perform certain governmental functions in the beach and harbor areas of the city only. Some of the functions are advisory with the City Council making the final decision. Others are final subject to an appeal to the City Council. An example of one of its advisory functions is reviewing and recommending a leasing arrangement between the city and a private developer. In the case of any lease the Council must authorize its execution. However, since the board is newly created, you cannot say whether the board's recommendation will be routinely accepted. An example of the board's discretionary function is granting conditional use permits in the harbor area. Essentially, the board functions as the "planning commission" for the city beach and harbor areas. Although the board is not involved in zoning or general planning in the harbor area, it does make important decisions affecting Gordon C. Phillips July 21, 1982 Page 2 individual developmental rights within its area of jurisdiction. Such decisions are final subject to appeal to the City Council. The problem, which prompts this request, is that there are three vacancies on this seven person board due to the lengthy screening process for new members. Financial conflict of interests have occurred involving the four members who have been named to date. There is no provision in the code for what happens if the board cannot obtain a quorum to enable it to take action. The questions, as you state them, are as follows: THE QUESTIONS 1. Does the "rule of necessity" apply, when vacancies could be filled by appointment by the City Council? Is there any mandate on the Council to fill such vacancies to avoid application of the "rule of necessity?" 2. Four members of the board is a quorum. Can a board member with a conflict of interest participate under the "rule of necessity" to form a quorum of the board, but abstain from the vote without affecting the quorum? 3. Does the "rule of necessity" apply when the board is exercising its advisory functions? ANALYSIS Government Code Section 871001/ prohibits a public official from making or participating in making a governmental decision in which he or she has a financial interest.
